metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the configuration mechanism for the await timeout (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_VERIFIED_TIMEOUT_SECS` env var, or a named config key in the existing settings object that `loop.py` already reads).
findings.finding: The 'configurable' timeout mechanism is unstated — an executor cannot act without knowing whether to read from an env var, a config file, a CLI flag, or a function parameter.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Bounded await: 'default 30 min, configurable'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Read from `AUTOLANDER_VERIFIED_TIMEOUT_SECS` env var (default 1800); fall back to the same source `land`'s timeout reads from.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rebar call signature: which id(s) to pass (e.g. `stack_id`, Gerrit change-id, or ticket number extracted from commit footer), and what 'annotate' means (e.g. post a comment with the merge SHA, set status to MERGED).
findings.finding: `import rebar` ticket close/annotate is under-specified: 'the right ids' and 'annotate' are undefined, so the executor cannot write the implementation or the unit test without guessing the rebar API contract.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit: 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops' and Tests: 'import rebar called with the right ids'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Call `rebar.ticket.close(ticket_id, merge_sha=tip_sha)` for each ticket linked in the commit footers of stack members; ticket_id is parsed from `Bug: <id>` footer lines.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live check that lands a real **two-member** chain end-to-end (exercising the per-member fresh-Verified rule with two distinct post-rebase SHAs), or document a named rationale for why the single-change live check is sufficient (e.g. 'multi-member path is covered by a scheduled integration suite in child ticket X').
findings.finding: The live end-to-end check covers only a single-change stack, leaving the plan's highest-risk path — multi-member chains where ALL members must carry a fresh per-member Verified on their own post-rebase SHA — exercised only by unit tests that can mock the Gerrit API and bypass the real enforcement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Tests: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the metric (e.g. `autolander.partial_land_errors` counter) and its emission mechanism (e.g. `metrics.increment('autolander.partial_land_errors')`), and add an assertion to the partial-land unit test that the counter was incremented exactly once.
findings.finding: 'AUTOLANDER_ERROR + metric' in the partial-land AC names no metric system, metric name, or proving command — an executor cannot verify the metric is emitted in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit: 'fails LOUDLY (`AUTOLANDER_ERROR` + metric)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ticket close/annotate via `import rebar` to the Why section's purpose statement and to the Files section's description of `loop.py` so this deliverable is covered by the plan body, not only by AC3.
findings.finding: The ticket close/annotate deliverable (`import rebar` ticket ops on merge) is specified only in AC3 but is absent from the plan body (Why section and Files section). The Why section describes only 'wait for CI to re-verify … and submit the tip atomically'; the Files section lists only `loop.py` with the description 'fresh-Verified await + ancestor-atomic submit'. The rebar ticket-ops work is an orphan criterion with no coverage in the plan body.
findings.location: AC3 ('On full-merge success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops') vs Why / Files sections
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section, append: '…and close/annotate associated tickets on successful merge (via `rebar` ticket ops).' In the Files section, update the `loop.py` line to: 'fresh-Verified await + ancestor-atomic submit + ticket close/annotate on merge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR (or CLAUDE.md / design doc section) capturing the fresh-Verified-per-member gate, the partial-land detection requirement, and the submitWholeTopic dependency as explicit architectural decisions with their rationale.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two novel architectural safety decisions — (1) the fresh-Verified-per-member gate as a hard submit invariant and (2) the partial-land detection + loud-failure pattern — but specifies no ADR or design-doc update, despite ADR-0041 already existing and being cited as precedent in this very plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Fresh Verified (per member)' and 'Ancestor-atomic submit' sections
findings.suggested_fix: Create ADR-00XX 'Autolander: fresh-Verified gate and atomic-submit safety invariants' covering: (a) why each chain member needs a fresh Verified on its own post-rebase SHA, (b) the partial-land loud-failure contract, and (c) the submitWholeTopic Gerrit config as a hard operational prerequisite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and mitigate the `change.submitWholeTopic` hard dependency (e.g., a startup config assertion or explicit pre-submit check) and document the `rebar` coupling with its expected interface contract and failure handling.
findings.finding: The `change.submitWholeTopic` Gerrit server config is silently assumed to be active ('active on this instance') with no acknowledgment, startup assertion, or mitigation plan if it is absent or later toggled off; and `import rebar` introduces a new cross-component dependency on the ticket-ops library with no coupling justification.
findings.location: AC: 'Ancestor-atomic submit' — 'change.submitWholeTopic active on this instance'; AC: 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via import rebar ticket ops'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preflight check in loop.py that reads the Gerrit server config and raises a loud error (AUTOLANDER_ERROR) if submitWholeTopic is not enabled; document the rebar ticket-ops interface (method names + expected exceptions) in a module-level docstring or the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the live end-to-end test (or add a second live fixture) to cover a real multi-member chain (≥2 changes), validating that all members reach MERGED atomically and that the partial-land detection correctly fires when one member is left open.
findings.finding: The live end-to-end test specified covers only 'one real single-change stack,' leaving the multi-member chain scenario — the plan's primary novel and highest-risk case (all members needing fresh Verified, atomic submit of a chain, partial-land detection) — without empirical validation against real Gerrit behavior.
findings.location: AC: 'Tests' — 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second live scenario: create a 2-change chain, trigger the autolander, and assert both changes reach MERGED in the same submit batch; separately inject a mock of Gerrit returning one change still OPEN after submit and assert AUTOLANDER_ERROR is raised and the stack is handed back.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff policy and explicit error-surfacing (metric/log) for the `import rebar` ticket-close call, and decide whether a rebar failure is fatal to the merge acknowledgment or is logged-and-continued.
findings.finding: The `import rebar` ticket-close/annotate call is an external dependency with no retry, backoff, or fallback specified. If the ticket system is down or returns an error after a successful merge, the plan does not say whether the error is surfaced, swallowed, or whether it blocks acknowledgment of the merge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit AC, last sentence: 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The rebar ticket-close call is retried up to N times with exponential backoff; on persistent failure it is logged at ERROR with an AUTOLANDER_TICKET_CLOSE_FAILED metric and the stack is still marked merged (non-blocking path), so a CI-down ticket system does not re-queue an already-merged stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements for the fresh-Verified await loop: at minimum, a metric counting await-timeouts (with stack_id label) and a log entry per member showing the waited duration and final SHA/Verified state at timeout or success.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify instrumentation (metrics/logs) for the fresh-Verified await loop itself — e.g., how long each member waited, whether timeout was hit, or how many polling iterations occurred — making it impossible for operators to distinguish 'CI is slow' from 'await is stuck' or to tune the 30-min default.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Bounded await AC: 'await fresh Verified up to a bounded timeout (default 30 min, configurable)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the check-then-act TOCTOU race: either (a) rely on a Gerrit submit requirement that enforces Verified +1 as a server-side gate (making the pre-check advisory), or (b) re-fetch Verified state immediately after a submit failure and before any retry, and document that a vote-revocation between check and submit will be caught by the post-submit member-status verification step if Gerrit rejects the submit.
findings.finding: There is a TOCTOU race between the fresh-Verified check (all members have Verified +1 on their post-rebase SHAs) and the submit call: a CI system could revoke a Verified vote between the check and the POST /submit, resulting in submitting an unverified tree. The plan has no re-verification or submit-guard for this window.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Fresh Verified AC + Ancestor-atomic submit AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retirement/cleanup path for per-stack await state on both the hand-back path and the successful-merge path, including whether stale SHA/timer records are explicitly cleared before re-entry.
findings.finding: The plan does not address what happens to the autolander's internal stack-tracking state after a hand-back (timeout or partial-land error): whether the stack entry is cleaned up, re-queued, or leaks. This is a shared-state retirement gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Bounded await AC and Ancestor-atomic submit AC (hand-back references)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live/integration end-to-end test (or explicitly defer and note it) covering a multi-member chain stack, demonstrating that submit is blocked while any member lacks a fresh post-rebase Verified and succeeds only once all members carry one.
findings.finding: The live end-to-end test in AC4 covers only a 'single-change stack', but the plan's primary invariant (AC1) is the multi-member chain case where all members must carry fresh Verified on their post-rebase SHAs. Without a live or integration-level test for a multi-member chain, the riskiest and most novel behavior has no end-state-observable proof beyond unit tests.
findings.location: AC4 — Tests: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the live-check AC to include a two-member chain scenario: push two related changes, trigger rebase:chain, confirm the await blocks until both carry fresh Verified on their new SHAs, then confirm both reach MERGED after submit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either reflect the ticket close/annotate concern in the plan title/Why (e.g. '… + post-merge ticket ops') and add `rebar` integration to the Files section, or explicitly call it a thin wrapper on an existing rebar helper so the scope is clear.
findings.finding: The title promises 'Fresh-Verified-per-member await + ancestor-atomic submit', but AC3 mandatorily includes ticket close/annotate via `import rebar` — a distinct integration with a separate system (the ticket tracker) that is not implied by either half of the title and introduces an independent reason-to-change (ticket system API changes).
findings.location: AC3 — 'On full-merge success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'; AC4 — 'ticket close/annotate on merge (`import rebar` called with the right ids)'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the title to 'Fresh-Verified-per-member await + ancestor-atomic submit + post-merge ticket ops' and add any rebar-related file(s) to the Files list; or add a note that rebar ticket ops are a one-liner call to an already-tested helper, scoping the risk down.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live/end-to-end acceptance criterion that lands a real **multi-member** (≥2 change) chain end-to-end, verifying: (a) submit is blocked until all members carry a fresh Verified on their post-rebase SHAs, (b) the atomic submit succeeds and every member reaches MERGED, and (c) partial-land is detected and triggers AUTOLANDER_ERROR when one member is forced open.
findings.finding: The live/end-to-end acceptance criterion is scoped exclusively to a 'single-change stack', leaving the multi-member chain path — the plan's own highest-risk scenario (per the R5 warning in AC Fresh Verified) — exercised only by unit tests that will necessarily mock Gerrit patchset-SHA and label responses. This is proxy-validation: the real Gerrit boundary (distinct post-rebase SHAs per member, per-member Verified label checks, 'Submitted Together' ancestry grouping, and partial-land detection) is never exercised live, so the suite can go green while the multi-member path is broken in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Tests bullet: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Tests AC bullet to read: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack AND one real ≥2-member chain end-to-end; the multi-member live test additionally verifies (a) hold-until-all-fresh (inject a CI delay on one member), and (b) partial-land detection (stub Gerrit to return one member open post-submit and assert AUTOLANDER_ERROR + hand-back).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Drop the 'matching `land`'s timeout' justification from AC2 and give the timeout config key a standalone name and default (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT_S`, default 1800 s) that does not presuppose a not-yet-existent `land` command; revisit alignment when S4 is built.
findings.finding: The bounded-await timeout is introduced as 'configurable, matching `land`'s timeout' — but `land` (the S4 agent/command this references) does not exist anywhere in the codebase. Tying the config key's raison d'être to a non-existent future component makes the 'matching' constraint a hypothetical YAGNI justification; the timeout itself is warranted, but naming its value as something to be kept in sync with an unbuilt peer creates premature config-surface coupling.
findings.location: AC2 — Bounded await: 'default 30 min, configurable, matching `land`'s timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'configurable, matching `land`'s timeout' with 'configurable via `AUTOLANDER_CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT_S` (default 1800 s)'; remove the forward-reference to `land`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify via a live Gerrit API call or cited doc that `POST /changes/{id}/rebase:chain` mints a new, distinct patchset SHA for **every** member of the chain (not just the tip), and record the evidence in an ADR or design note linked from this AC.
findings.finding: The plan confidently asserts 'rebase:chain mints a NEW patchset (distinct SHA) per member' as the premise for the all-members-fresh-Verified rule. This is an empirical claim about the Gerrit rebase:chain REST endpoint's per-member behavior. No repo artifact (docs, ADR, test output, or existing code) verifies this; infra/autolander/ does not exist, and prior S2a plan-review cache entries also flag rebase:chain as unverified on this instance. If rebase:chain does NOT increment every member's patchset number (e.g., it only updates the tip), the entire per-member fresh-Verified gate is based on a false premise and the R5 invariant could be silently violated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Fresh Verified (per member)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test fixture or cite a Gerrit 3.14.x release note / API doc confirming that rebase:chain increments each member's patchset. Alternatively, make the per-member fresh check compare the current-patchset SHA against the SHA recorded by S2a after the rebase call, rather than relying on the patchset-minting assumption.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the autolander S3 story by ticket ID, confirm it includes topic-assignment (`stack_id` → Gerrit topic) as an explicit AC, and add it as a `depends_on` link (or inline the topic-set call into S2c if S3 is not planned).
findings.finding: The ancestor-atomic submit AC states the submit's all-or-nothing guarantee is 'reinforced by the shared topic S3 assigns to the stack (`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic)'. This is a descoping dependency: the topic-assignment work is attributed to a sibling autolander story 'S3', but no ticket ID is cited, no repo file contains `stack_id`, and `infra/autolander/` does not exist. If S3 has not yet assigned topics (or is never built), the `submitWholeTopic` reinforcement is missing and partial-land risk increases — yet this plan's partial-land detection is the only guard, and that detection relies on a post-hoc check rather than preventing the partial submit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit (mandatory)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mark `infra/autolander/loop.py` as NEW (not an edit) in the Files section, and explicitly state that S2c's additions depend on S2a's Gerrit helper and S2b's wipChain state machine already being present in `loop.py` (i.e., declare the sequencing contract with S2a and S2b).
findings.finding: `infra/autolander/loop.py` does not exist (the directory `infra/autolander/` is absent from the repo), yet the plan's Files section lists it without marking it as a NEW creation. More critically, sibling tasks S2a (89f9-b381-60e2-4802) and S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee) also both target `infra/autolander/loop.py` as their edit target, meaning three sibling tasks write to the same file in parallel. The plan does not acknowledge this shared-file coordination requirement.
findings.location: ## Files — `infra/autolander/loop.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '`infra/autolander/loop.py` — NEW file (created by S2a; S2c adds the fresh-Verified await loop and ancestor-atomic submit). Sequencing: S2a must land before S2c; S2b's wipChain state machine is a prerequisite (add `depends_on: [89f9-b381-60e2-4802, a9e3-580c-409c-4eee]` to the ticket deps).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the topic-assignment story ('S3 assigns `stack_id` as the Gerrit topic') as a formal `depends_on` prerequisite, or clarify that `submitWholeTopic` is purely opportunistic reinforcement and the post-submit member-status check is the mandatory gate (AC3 already says this, but the framing of topic as an active reinforcement creates an undeclared dependency).
findings.finding: AC3 states the ancestor-atomic submit is reinforced by 'the shared topic S3 assigns to the stack (`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic)' — but this 'S3' (the autolander conflict/hand-back story, distinct from the Gerrit-setup story) is not in this ticket's `deps`, and `stack_id`/topic-assignment does not exist anywhere in the current codebase. The `submitWholeTopic` safety net in AC3 thus depends on a topic-assignment deliverable from an unenumerated upstream task that is never declared as a prerequisite.
findings.location: AC3 — Ancestor-atomic submit: 'shared topic S3 assigns to the stack (`stack_id` = the Gerrit topic)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `depends_on: a9e3-580c-409c-4eee` (S2b) to this ticket's deps, since AC2's timeout path explicitly invokes S2b's hand-back mechanism.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'S2b mechanism' for the timeout hand-back path, but S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee) is not listed in this ticket's `deps` — the dependency on S2b's hand-back implementation is implicit only through the shared parent story (S2). This means S2c has an unenumerated caller/consumer relationship: it calls into S2b's hand-back logic but declares no formal ordering dependency on S2b.
findings.location: AC2 — Bounded await: 'hand the stack back (S2b mechanism)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ticket deps: `depends_on: [a9e3-580c-409c-4eee]` and note in the Files section that the hand-back invocation reuses the function/helper introduced by S2b.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the atomicity-mechanism framing in the Ancestor-atomic submit AC: state that `submitWholeTopic` (keyed on the Gerrit topic S3 assigns) is the PRIMARY all-or-nothing mechanism per ADR-0040, that relation-chain ancestry establishes the commit graph (not the atomicity guarantee), and add an explicit pre-submit check that the Gerrit topic is set on every chain member before calling `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit`.
findings.finding: The plan inverts the causal primacy of the two atomicity mechanisms, attributing all-or-nothing submission to 'relation-chain ancestry' first and `submitWholeTopic` as a mere 'reinforcement.' ADR-0040 is explicit: R3 (atomic stack landing) is delivered by `change.submitWholeTopic = true` — 'a topic's linear changes submit atomically.' Gerrit relation-chain ancestry alone does NOT guarantee that submitting the tip submits all ancestors atomically; `submitWholeTopic` (keyed on the Gerrit topic S3 assigns) is the real grouping mechanism. An implementer who reads the plan's framing literally might believe relation-chain ancestry is sufficient and treat the topic assignment as secondary insurance, building a subtly under-specified data-flow where the topic is not reliably set before submit is called.
findings.location: AC: Ancestor-atomic submit — 'Gerrit submits the chain's members "Submitted Together" by relation-chain ancestry (all-or-nothing), reinforced by the shared topic S3 assigns to the stack'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC sentence as: 'Ancestor-atomic submit = a single `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` on the chain tip. The all-or-nothing guarantee is provided by `change.submitWholeTopic` (active on this instance, per ADR-0040) keyed on the `stack_id` Gerrit topic S3 assigns; relation-chain ancestry ensures Gerrit groups the commits but does not alone provide the all-or-nothing constraint. **Pre-submit check (mandatory):** verify every chain member carries the expected `stack_id` topic before calling submit, so `submitWholeTopic` can group them correctly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Bounded-await AC explaining why polling (at the parent's 15s cadence) is chosen for the Verified-await step rather than a Gerrit SSH stream or webhook (e.g., simplicity, codebase-alignment with the rest of the loop, avoids a persistent SSH connection).
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why the Verified-await is implemented as a poll (inherited from the parent's 15s cadence) rather than as a Gerrit SSH event-stream listener or webhook consumer. The parent story (S2) marks the 15s poll cadence 'design-fixed' but S2c's own text contains no rationale for the polling approach for this specific step — an absence-of-rationale finding per G6(4).
findings.location: AC: Bounded await — 'await fresh Verified up to a bounded timeout (default 30 min, configurable, matching `land`'s timeout)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability claim: cite a Gerrit REST API documentation reference (or a captured live API response / e2e test result) confirming that `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` atomically submits all ancestor relation-chain members on this Gerrit version, OR clarify that `submitWholeTopic` is the sole atomicity mechanism and relation-chain ancestry is supplementary.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Gerrit submits the chain's members "Submitted Together" by relation-chain ancestry (all-or-nothing)' as the primary mechanism for ancestor-atomic submit via a single `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` on the tip. However, ADR-0040 attributes R3 (atomic chain landing) exclusively to `change.submitWholeTopic = true`, not to relation-chain ancestry. The existing `submit_change()` helper in `infra/gerrit/feature-branch-e2e.sh` calls `POST /changes/$1/submit` on individual changes, not on a chain tip — providing no evidence that a tip-only submit atomically pulls in ancestors via relation-chain semantics as a standalone Gerrit capability. The plan names both mechanisms but makes 'relation-chain ancestry' primary without a cited Gerrit documentation reference, a captured API response, or any repo test confirming this behavior.
findings.location: AC: Ancestor-atomic submit — 'Gerrit submits the chain's members "Submitted Together" by relation-chain ancestry (all-or-nothing)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'by relation-chain ancestry (all-or-nothing)' with a more precise statement: 'Gerrit's submitWholeTopic=true (confirmed active in infra/compose/gerrit.config) causes all topic-sharing members to submit atomically when the tip is submitted; relation-chain ancestry provides additional grouping. Confirmed by [cite Gerrit docs URL or e2e test].' If relation-chain ancestry is independently load-bearing, add a unit/e2e test that proves it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and explicitly state that `rebar` (or the specific sub-package needed for ticket close/annotate) is a declared runtime dependency of the `infra/autolander` service, and identify where that dependency is declared (e.g., a requirements file, a pyproject extras section, or the container image spec).
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops' as part of the merge-success path, but does not establish that the `rebar` library is a declared runtime dependency of the `infra/autolander` service. The parent ticket (S2, perovskite-frilly-grebe) explicitly positions the autolander as 'project infra — NOT src/rebar core', and `infra/autolander/` has no existing files (directory absent from repo). No requirements file, pyproject.toml section, or Dockerfile for the autolander is referenced that would confirm `import rebar` resolves at runtime in the autolander container.
findings.location: AC: Ancestor-atomic submit — 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact Gerrit API query and field comparison used to determine that a `Verified +1` is 'fresh' (i.e., cast on the post-rebase patchset SHA): name the endpoint (e.g., `GET /changes/{id}/detail?o=DETAILED_LABELS&o=CURRENT_REVISION`), the response field(s) to inspect (e.g., `labels.Verified.all[].value` filtered by `_revision_number == currentPatchSet.number`), and how the expected SHA is sourced from the rebase:chain response.
findings.finding: The 'fresh Verified +1 on the exact rebased patchset SHA' gate is the plan's core safety invariant, but the decision rule — which Gerrit API field to query, how to obtain the current patchset number post-rebase, and how to confirm a vote is fresh (not carried) — is never specified co-located with that gate. An executor must invent the Gerrit API surface (e.g., DETAILED_LABELS + patchset revision matching, or currentPatchSet.approvals) and the freshness-detection logic from scratch.
findings.location: AC1: Fresh Verified (per member)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, add a protocol box: 'After rebase:chain, record {change_id → new_patchset_sha} for each member. Poll GET /changes/{id}/detail?o=DETAILED_LABELS; a vote is fresh iff labels["Verified"].all contains an entry with value=1 whose _revision_number equals the patchset number corresponding to the new_patchset_sha.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate with AC3 the exact `import rebar` call(s) required on merge success: name the function(s) (e.g., `rebar.transition(ticket_id, target='closed')` and/or `rebar.comment(ticket_id, body)`), specify where ticket IDs are sourced (e.g., `Rebar-Ticket:` commit footer, stack metadata field), and state the annotation body template.
findings.finding: The 'close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops' instruction in AC3 never specifies: (a) which rebar API call(s) to invoke (rebar.transition? rebar.comment? both?), (b) where the ticket IDs originate (from the stack metadata? from a change's commit message? from a field set by S3?), or (c) what annotation text to write. This is a processing protocol without a co-located invocation schema.
findings.location: AC3: Ancestor-atomic submit — 'On full-merge success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the phase value(s) this plan adds to the autolander phase enum (e.g., `awaiting_verified`) and confirm they are registered in the shared enum/schema owned by S2b, so observability (S5) and crash recovery (S5d) can key on them correctly.
findings.finding: The plan introduces an `awaiting_verified` (or equivalent) operational phase in the autolander state machine but never names this phase value or specifies where it belongs in the phase enum. The `phase` enum vocabulary is established by sibling plans (S2b owns the enum; S5-series plans reference it), and this plan's await loop implicitly requires a new phase entry without declaring it.
findings.location: AC2: Bounded await / implicit phase state; cross-referenced by sibling plan cache entries for S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee) and S5 (3c55-2af3-0c9c-4b11)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the hand-back interface inline (or cite the exact function/symbol from S2b that this plan must call), including its call signature and what state the loop must be in before invoking it, so the timeout and partial-land fallbacks are implementable from this plan alone.
findings.finding: The timeout fallback 'hand the stack back (S2b mechanism)' is the only specified sub-step fallback for the await loop, but the interface contract of the S2b mechanism is never defined in this plan. An executor cannot implement the hand-back call without knowing the function signature, the data handed back, or the state transition required — all of which live exclusively in a sibling plan.
findings.location: AC2: Bounded await — 'hand the stack back (S2b mechanism)'

findings.finding: The ticket close/annotate step ('close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops') in AC3 has no associated test or proving command anywhere in the Tests AC, leaving this behavior unverified by the plan.
findings.location: AC: Ancestor-atomic submit (third bullet) — 'On success, close/annotate the associated ticket(s) via `import rebar` ticket ops'

findings.finding: The live end-to-end check covers only a 'single-change stack', but the plan's core invariant (AC1) is the multi-member chain case where ALL members must carry a fresh Verified on their post-rebase SHAs. Under E6(f), the new multi-member fresh-Verified code path is never exercised end-to-end in production conditions — only via unit tests that can be satisfied with mocks/stubs. Green unit tests plus a signed completion verdict are not sufficient to prove the chain-wide invariant holds on the live Gerrit instance.
findings.location: AC: Tests (last bullet) — 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Tests AC: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end AND one real two-member chain stack where member[0] initially has no fresh Verified, CI completes, then the stack submits atomically via a single tip submit.'

findings.finding: The 'Ancestor-atomic submit' AC specifies closing/annotating associated tickets via `import rebar` ticket ops on success, but the Tests AC has no corresponding test for this behavior — it only covers the all-members-fresh rule, the bounded-await timeout→hand-back, and a live single-change-stack land. Ticket-op correctness (including the case where submit succeeds but rebar call fails) is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: AC: 'Ancestor-atomic submit' vs AC: 'Tests'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests AC, append: 'unit-test that on a successful submit the associated ticket(s) are closed/annotated via rebar, and that a rebar failure after a successful submit is surfaced (not silently ignored).'

findings.finding: The live end-to-end check is scoped to 'one real single-change stack', leaving the novel and risky multi-member chain scenario (all members must carry fresh Verified on distinct post-rebase SHAs before a single tip submit) without empirical validation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Tests' bullet: 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'

findings.finding: The Tests AC specifies no test whatsoever for the `import rebar` ticket close/annotate behavior that is triggered on successful submit. This new user-facing side-effect (ticket is closed/annotated) has neither a happy-path unit test nor a failure-path test (e.g., rebar call fails, ticket already closed, no associated ticket).
findings.location: AC: 'Ancestor-atomic submit' bullet and 'Tests' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests AC, add: 'unit-test that a successful submit triggers the rebar ticket-close/annotate call (assert call is made with correct args); and that a rebar failure is logged and does not crash the loop'.

findings.finding: The live end-to-end check covers only a single-change stack. The defining new behavior of this ticket — iterating over ALL chain members to verify each holds a fresh Verified +1 on its own post-rebase SHA — is exercised live only for N=1, where the per-member iteration loop trivially runs once. The multi-member fresh-Verified path (N≥2) is tested exclusively through unit/mock coverage, which presumably stubs the Gerrit label-query call and never hits the real API.
findings.location: AC: 'Tests' bullet — 'a live check lands one real single-change stack end-to-end'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Tests AC: 'a live check also verifies a two-member chain: (i) submit is held while member-2 lacks a fresh Verified after rebase, (ii) submit proceeds once both members have fresh Verified, (iii) both changes appear merged atomically.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that a single `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` on the chain tip 'submits the tip together with all its ancestors atomically' via 'relation-chain semantics'. This is mechanistically incorrect as stated: Gerrit's atomic multi-change submit relies on `submitWholeTopic = true` (confirmed active in the live config and ADR-0040), which operates on shared *topic*, not relation-chain ancestry. Relation-chain ancestry and topic membership are orthogonal in Gerrit. If chain members do not share a topic, submitting the tip leaves ancestors in the 'open' state — a partial stack land that violates R3 and potentially R5. The plan never asserts that `rebase:chain` assigns a shared topic or that the autolander verifies topic membership before submitting.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit (AC3)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical in AC3 with: 'under FFO + `submitWholeTopic = true` (already enabled server-wide), submitting the tip atomically lands all chain members only if they share a common topic — the autolander MUST verify all members carry the same topic before submit, and must assign/assert the topic at queue-entry time.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'under FFO with relation-chain semantics this submits the tip together with all its ancestors atomically (all-or-nothing)' — but ADR-0040 attributes the multi-change atomic landing guarantee (R3) to `change.submitWholeTopic = true`, not to relation-chain submit semantics. These are distinct Gerrit mechanisms: a bare `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` may or may not propagate to relation-chain ancestors in an all-or-nothing manner; the codebase contains no e2e test or ADR passage that confirms this for a multi-member relation chain. The plan treats this as a verified fact without grounding.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit bullet: 'a single `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` on the chain tip; under FFO with relation-chain semantics this submits the tip together with all its ancestors atomically (all-or-nothing)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note (or ADR amendment) clarifying that the atomic ancestor submit relies on `change.submitWholeTopic = true` (already set server-wide) with all chain members sharing a common topic — OR cite the Gerrit REST API docs showing that `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` propagates to relation-chain ancestors without topic involvement. If both mechanisms apply, name both explicitly.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `POST /changes/{tip-id}/submit` causes ancestor-atomic ('all-or-nothing') submit via 'relation-chain semantics' under FFO. This is a NEW endpoint surface not implemented anywhere in the codebase (gerrit_client.py has no submit method). The all-or-nothing ancestor-atomic claim for a single tip submit in a relation chain — as distinct from topic-based atomicity (`submitWholeTopic = true`) — is an unverified Gerrit capability claim. In practice, Gerrit submits relation-chain ancestors when submitting a tip, but the all-or-nothing guarantee (vs. partial submit if some ancestor is independently submittable) depends on server behavior that is not confirmed in any repo artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Ancestor-atomic submit AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment in the implementation citing the Gerrit REST API docs section on submit (rest-api-changes.html#submit-change), and confirm via a live integration test or Gerrit source that relation-chain ancestors are included atomically. If they are not, wrap ancestor submits in a topic and rely on `submitWholeTopic = true` instead.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
